summary: Asparouhova reflects on Y Combinator's Female Founder Conference, praising its deliberate avoidance of typical 'women in tech' topics like sexism and work-life balance. Instead, the conference focused purely on how to build companies, allowing speakers to emerge as multidimensional individuals rather than representatives of their gender. She argues that creating separate sectors for women, minorities, or social impact actually makes people one-dimensional and works against true inclusion. The post advocates for breaking down identity-based silos rather than building new ones.
key_points: The conference's refusal to focus on gendered topics allowed speakers to be seen as individuals with unique perspectives rather than 'women founders'
Building identity-based sectors (women entrepreneurship, social entrepreneurship, etc.) is counterproductive because it flattens people into one-dimensional categories
Exclusion is multidimensional—engineers vs non-engineers, introverts vs extroverts—and can't be solved by simple demographic categories
Not every female founder wants to be known primarily as a female founder; some simply want to be recognized for excellence among all peers
True inclusion comes from breaking down sectors, not creating new siloed ones
money_quotes: Building sectors is dangerous because it makes us one-dimensional.
Not every female founder wants to be known as a female founder. Not every female VC wants to be known as a female VC.
We are awash in sectors that are ostensibly trying to make business better. Social impact has become siloed.
By not affixing the same, flat, 'women X' label onto everyone, the Female Founder Conference allowed everyone in attendance to breathe as themselves.
A straight white male may still feel left out for not being technical, being the quiet one in a room, or being relegated to a corner of the office.
Am I creating new sectors, or helping to break them down?
key_insight: True inclusion comes not from building identity-based sectors that flatten people into categories, but from breaking them down so individuals can be recognized for their unique qualities and accomplishments.
